This is TALK OF THE NATION.
I'm Neal Conan in Washington.
Later in the program, politics in Afghanistan and an argument that at least parts of the Taliban have to share power there, but first, political junkie Ken Rudin is with us, as he is every Wednesday.
You can read his blog and listen to his podcast if you go to npr.org.
And we now focus on the politics of Afghanistan.

President Obama knew that the surge would be unpopular with the left of the Democratic Party that forms an important part of his political base, but he made sure to specify an exit strategy in his speech at West Point.
I have determined that it is in our vital national interest to send an additional 30,000 U.S.
troops to Afghanistan.
After 18 months, our troops will begin to come home.
But that timetable seemed to slip by the time administration officials appeared on the Sunday talk shows.
This is Secretary of State Hillary Clinton on NBC's ""Meet the Press.
(Soundbite of television program, ""Meet the Press"")
We're not talking about an exit strategy or a drop-dead deadline.
What we're talking about is an assessment that in January, 2011, we can begin a transition, a transition to hand off responsibility of the Afghan forces.
And this week, the military commander in Afghanistan also stressed the importance of waiting until a year from January before evaluating the surge.
We want to hear from progressives in the audience today.
What message does this send to the anti-war activists who helped candidate Obama in the Democratic primaries?
Are you willing to give the president the benefit of the doubt now?
Give us a call, 800-989-8255.
Email us, talk@npr.org.
You can also join our conversation on our Web site.
That's at npr.org, click on TALK OF THE NATION.
And joining us here in Studio 3A is Faiz Shakir, a research director at the Center for American Progress and editor-in-chief of thinkprogress.org.
Thanks very much for coming in today.
Thanks for having me.
And what do you take from the president's speech and then the apparent switch by Sunday?
Well, it's clear that he was trying to talk to two different audiences in the speech.
So on the one sentence, he said that we're not going to withdraw, we're going to stay there.
We've got to commit 30,000 extra troops to try to win this effort.
And that's clearly an effort to win over the people who are proponents of staying in Afghanistan.
But then in the very next sentence, it's telling that he says we're going to leave in 18 months.
So I think he's trying to draw in his progressive base that's very concerned that there isn't a recipe for success, particularly through the addition of more troops.

And I think that over the course of the weekend, you see some of his more hawkish military advisors - and you also played some clips from Secretary Clinton - who are, I think trying to shape the rhetorical terrain so that it will create the breathing space, if you will, for them to politically stay longer, if necessary.
Well, do you think they were going beyond what the president wanted them to say?
Let's go next to Nate(ph), Nate with us from Avon in Colorado.
Hey, there.
I feel like my progressive base is being somewhat dishonest, in a way.
I mean, Obama, throughout the campaign and even into his presidency, always said he was going to adjust his focus on Afghanistan.
It was always one of the things he would talk about.
And I don't know why my base is just freaking out, I mean, as though he - are they pretending that he did not say these things and wishing that he didn't say these things?
I think that they are instituting some of their own desires to keep as much flexibility in the war plan as much as possible.
The president, of course, has his own political considerations.

I don't mean to suggest that they were the reason he instituted his plan, but I think the rhetoric was shaped in such a way to make sure that he could mollify the various audiences that he was speaking to.
But they have a very different constituency.
So you see the military saying well, if we need to stay past 20 months, then we've got to start using rhetoric right now that's going to allow us to stay.

So the secretary of state, appointed by the president of the United States, the head of the National Security Council, who serves at the president's pleasure and does not have to be confirmed by the Senate or anybody else, those two people who appeared on the Sunday talk shows, much less General McChrystal, Admiral Mullen and the ambassador to Kabul, all of whom serve at the pleasure of the president, are going beyond what the White House told them to say?
I think that they're trying to ensure that they have the rhetorical space to continue with the war plan beyond 18 months.
And I think it's a shame that they feel like there's still an internal struggle that's playing out in public.
I think that they had debated this for months, I think at least three months, hopefully had come to terms with the fact that they were going to begin a withdrawal at 18 months.

But I think that there are still some elements of the administration, particularly in the military - I think General Petraeus and General Jim Jones, his national security advisor, amongst others, who are saying that this will - this may begin, but we're going to keep 100,000 troops there even beyond 18 months, so be prepared.
And I think that that's sending the wrong message and undercutting what President Obama had to say to the West Point cadets.
When all he would have to do is tell his press secretary, Robert Gibbs, to say wait a minute, that's not quite right.
We're going to start pulling out in 18 months.
And at some point, it may come to that, but right now, I think that they're still trying to gauge the political fallout from all this.
I think on the one hand, the policy is set in stone.
They feel like in 18 months, they're going to start to begin a withdrawal.
But that policy is being shaped rhetorically in so many different ways, and I think that that's what they need to start getting under control.
And I'm concerned that the rhetoric of those who have intentions of staying longer may outweigh and voice over those who would like to see that withdrawal begin in 18 months.
Including their boss, the president.
Ken?

When the president said at West Point - he was talking about a timetable for July - begin withdrawing troops in July, 2001, immediately people like John McCain said this is, you know, this is giving a signal to al-Qaida and the Taliban that you don't want to do that, you don't want to do a timetable to the enemy.
And then as we heard, we heard the administration figures speaking over the weekend that perhaps gave some ease to the McCains.
What ease has the left, the progressives heard, from the White House since the West Point speech?
You know, I think coming out of the West Point speech, there were progressives who were heartened to see what they thought was an exit strategy articulated by the president.
They didn't want to call it that, but it seemed clear that 18 months was going to begin the end of our presence in Afghanistan.
It was clear that the president said we're not going to stay forever.
No blank check.
No blank check, and we're going to tell the Afghanistan people we're not occupiers, we're ready to leave.
Then, you know, and I think that that - the president even boxed him in in that speech, boxed himself in in that speech.

Because if you look at the 18 months timetable, he's boxing himself politically because he will be up for re-election at a time when he will actually have to decide whether he's going to hold to that 18 months or not.
And if he does not, he risks, of course, fracturing and alienating the very progressive base that helped put him in power.
Let's get some callers in on the conversation, 800-989-8255.
Email talk@npr.org.
Andre(ph) is with us from Oakland.
Yes, I think that we don't need to give the president the benefit of the doubt.
We need to give him a piece of our mind.
Just because he said it, it sounds like it's a turd that's dipped in some honey, and he's very good at framing it.
But he needs to know that his progressive base is upset, and you need to end the war.
How long are we going to be there?
And if it's not going to end, just tell the people: We're going to be here forever.
Let's start building military bases, and just say that, rather than all this oh, the (unintelligible) talking about well, we need - there's no timetable.
We need to evaluate.
Well, we've been evaluating this war since, what, 2003?
How long does it take to evaluate it?
At least since 2001, anyway, when it started.

Afghanistan started in 2001, probably, right?
When did we start with them?
Indeed.
Yeah, no this is - we're talking about Afghanistan here.
When did we start with them?
Jesus.
Yeah, it's been a long time, eight years.
It's time to end the war.
Thanks very much for the call, Andre.
And he represents the view of people like Congressman McGovern of Massachusetts, who says wait a minute, we're going to have a vote.
We should stop funding this.
We should tell the president no. We need to start pulling these troops out now.
Right.
And there's a cost-benefits issue here, particularly as we're debating health care, that you have $100 billion that either goes to Afghanistan or either goes to health care for all Americans.
And I think that for many progressive activists, they see that as an easy cost benefit.
Let's invest here at home.
The other thing I'd say is - to defend Obama just very quickly - I think he saw the past eight years in Afghanistan as a lack of investment and a lack of a strategy for success.
And I think he's saying to himself and to his advisors: Let's give this one shot.
We've got 18 months.
Let's give it one shot and then see what happens from that.
I don't feel bad at all.
I think he's doing exactly what he said, and I'm glad he's focusing more on Afghanistan now.
And Nate is correct.
That is what the president campaigned on.
The president certainly campaigned on that.
He also campaigned on withdrawing from Iraq.
We see that the rhetoric and the policy changes over time, due to realities on the ground.

And I think many progressives said that the reality in Afghanistan is changing and that the president's view of the policy and the cost that that policy would inflict on his administration needed to change, as well.
And I think the president went through a three-month process where I think he said he listened to all points of view.
